subject: math
title: PSLE Math — Hidden Constant Total Trap

stats.headline: Many PSLE word problems hide an invariant: a TOTAL, a DIFFERENCE, or one quantity that secretly never changes — or two parties whose totals are secretly equal. Spot the invariant and the problem becomes a 2-line ratio solve. Miss it and you chase the wrong unknown.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Hidden Constant Total
keyConcepts.body: Some word problems describe stamps being given away, money moving between people, or beads being removed from a box. Your brain wants to redo the ratios from scratch — but usually, ONE thing in the problem never changes. Find that invariant first.
bullets:
**The 4 patterns :**
-- 🔵 **Pattern A — TOTAL stays the same** — one person gives to another (internal transfer) -- 🟢 **Pattern B — DIFFERENCE stays the same** — both quantities grow or shrink by the SAME amount -- 🟣 **Pattern C — ONE quantity stays the same** — the problem only acts on the OTHER quantity -- 🟠 **Pattern D — TWO situations share an EQUAL TOTAL** — different ratios → equalise the totals first
💡 ** ** Pattern A in disguise
the **3-question check**

**The 4 patterns to watch for:**
-- 🔵 **Pattern A — TOTAL stays the same** — happens when one person gives to another (internal transfer).
-- 🟢 **Pattern B — DIFFERENCE stays the same** — happens when both quantities grow or shrink by the SAME amount.
-- 🟣 **Pattern C — ONE quantity stays the same** — happens when the problem only acts on the OTHER quantity.
-- 🟠 **Pattern D — TWO situations share an EQUAL TOTAL** — with different ratios → equalise the totals first.

💡 **Big insight:** "give-and-take" problems (e.g. "A gave $X to B, now A has $Y less than B") are just Pattern A in disguise — the TOTAL is still constant. The "difference shifts by 2 × transfer" is the SAME unlock, framed differently.
On the next slide — the **3-question check** that figures out WHICH pattern the problem is hiding.

keyConcepts.title: The 3-question check — every before/after problem
keyConcepts.body: Before you draw any model or write any ratio, run these 3 questions on the problem. They take 15 seconds and they tell you which pattern (A, B, C, or D) you're facing.
bullets:
**Q1 — Where does the stuff GO?**
-- one person/box to ANOTHER person/box **internal transfer** → **TOTAL is constant** (Pattern A) -- total drops

**Q2 — How much was removed from EACH part?**
-- the SAME amount **DIFFERENCE is constant** (Pattern B) -- **THE OTHER is the constant** (Pattern C)

**Q3 — Are there TWO situations with equal totals but different ratios?**
-- the same TOTAL different ratios **Pattern D**
💡 one and only one

Question **Q1 — Where does the stuff GO?**
-- If it moves from one person/box to ANOTHER person/box in the SAME problem → it's an **internal transfer** → **TOTAL is constant** (Pattern A). This includes give-and-take like "A gave $X to B."
-- If it leaves the problem entirely (eaten, sold, removed) → total drops — check Q2.

Question **Q2 — How much was removed from EACH part?**
-- If the SAME amount was removed from both → **DIFFERENCE is constant** (Pattern B).
-- If only ONE part lost or gained → **THE OTHER is the constant** (Pattern C).

Question **Q3 — Are there TWO situations with equal totals but different ratios?**
-- If two boxes / two people share the same TOTAL but different ratios → **Pattern D** — equalise the totals before combining or comparing.
💡 Most PSLE problems fit one and only one of A-D. Identify it first, write your equation second.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Internal transfer (TOTAL is constant)
keyConcepts.body: The headline pattern. Clue words: "gave some to", "transferred", "moved from … to", "passed". The stuff doesn't leave the problem — it just moves sides. Add the BEFORE quantities once and use that as your fixed total. Includes give-and-take problems!
bullets:
**📝 Worked example 1 :**
Sally had **40 stamps**, Ting had **32 stamps**. Sally gave some to Ting. **1 : 2**.

Total = 40 + 32 = **72 stamps** After ratio: 1 : 2 → 3 units = 72 → 1 unit = **24**.
Ting = 2 units = **48 stamps**

**📝 Worked example 2 :**
Ken had **$15 more** than Lisa Ken gave some money to Lisa. Ken had **$21 less** than Lisa
** ** Total swing in gap = 15 + 21 = **$36** Transfer = 36 ÷ 2 = **$18**

**📝 Worked example 1 — after-ratio version:**
Sally had **40 stamps**, Ting had **32 stamps**.
Then, Sally gave some to Ting.
Afterwards, the ratio of Sally to Ting was **1 : 2**.
How many stamps did Ting have in the end?

Total = 40 + 32 = **72 stamps** — fixed by internal transfer.
After ratio: 1 : 2 → 3 units = 72 → 1 unit = **24**.
Ting = 2 units = **48 stamps**.

**📝 Worked example 2 — give-and-take version, SAME pattern:**
Ken had **$15 more** than Lisa.
Then, Ken gave some money to Lisa.
Now, Ken had **$21 less** than Lisa.
How much did Ken give?

**The give-and-take shortcut:** each $1 transferred shifts the gap by $2 (Ken loses $1, Lisa gains $1).
Total swing in gap = 15 + 21 = **$36** (crossed zero from +15 to −21).
Transfer = 36 ÷ 2 = **$18**.

⚠️ **Common wrong moves:** (1) Treating the transfer as an external loss and shrinking the total — DON'T. (2) Computing $21 − $15 = $6 and stopping — IGNORES the doubling. Both errors miss that the TOTAL pile is conserved.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Equal change to both (DIFFERENCE is constant)
keyConcepts.body: Clue words: "equal number", "the same number of … and …", "removed [X] of each". When BOTH parts change by the SAME amount, the GAP between them stays exactly the same. That gap is your anchor.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example:**
Susan had blue and red beads in the ratio **7 : 13** She removed an **equal number** of blue and red beads.
The new ratio became **1 : 3** If she removed **20 blue beads**, how many beads of each colour were there at first?

** **
Equal removed → DIFFERENCE is fixed.
Before difference = 13u − 7u = **6 units**

** **
After: 1 : 3 → difference = 2 parts.
6 old units = 2 new parts → **1 new part = 3 old units**.
After ratio in old units: 3u (blue) : 9u (red)

** **
Blue removed = 7u − 3u = **4 units** = 20 → **1 unit = 5** Original blue = 7u = **35 beads**. Original red = 13u = **65 beads**
⚠️ ** ** difference

**📝 Worked example:**
Susan had blue and red beads in the ratio **7 : 13**.
Then, She removed an **equal number** of blue and red beads.
The new ratio became **1 : 3**.
If she removed **20 blue beads**, how many beads of each colour were there at first?

**Step 1 — Spot the invariant.**
Equal removed → DIFFERENCE is fixed.
Before difference = 13u − 7u = **6 units** (old units).

**Step 2 — Match the difference in the new ratio.**
After: 1 : 3 → difference = 2 parts.
6 old units = 2 new parts → **1 new part = 3 old units**.
After ratio in old units: 3u (blue) : 9u (red).

**Step 3 — Solve.**
Blue removed = 7u − 3u = **4 units** = 20 → **1 unit = 5**.
Original blue = 7u = **35 beads**. Original red = 13u = **65 beads**.
(Check: 35 : 65 → remove 20 each → 15 : 45 = 1 : 3 ✓)
⚠️ **Common wrong move:** students assume the TOTAL is constant — but beads were REMOVED, so total dropped. The difference is what survived equal removal, not the total.

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Internal transfer & Equal removal

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern A — Internal transfer
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Ahmad had 60 marbles and Bala had 20 marbles. Ahmad gave some
marbles to Bala. After that, the ratio of Ahmad's marbles to
Bala's marbles was 3 : 5. How many marbles did Ahmad give to Bala?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Internal transfer → TOTAL is constant = 60 + 20 = 80. After: 3 : 5 → 8 units = 80 → 1 unit = 10. Ahmad now has 3u = 30. He started with 60, so he gave 60 − 30 = 30 marbles.

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern B — Equal removal
interactiveQuiz.stem:
A box had green and yellow buttons in the ratio 4 : 9. An equal
number of green and yellow buttons were taken out. The new
ratio became 1 : 3. If 15 green buttons were taken out, how
many yellow buttons were left in the box?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Equal removal → DIFFERENCE is fixed. Before: 4u : 9u → difference = 5u. After: 1p : 3p → difference = 2p. So 5u = 2p, meaning 1p = 2.5u. Green left = 1p = 2.5u, so green removed = 4u − 2.5u = 1.5u = 15 → 1u = 10. Yellow left = 3p = 7.5u = 75 buttons. (Check: start 40 : 90, remove 15 each → 25 : 75 = 1 : 3 ✓)

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — One quantity unchanged
keyConcepts.body: Clue: the problem only acts on ONE of the two parts. The OTHER part is your anchor — it never changes, so treat it as a constant.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example:**
Mei Ling has **90 books** in total — some story, the rest reference.
She gave away **2/3 of her story books**.
She did NOT give away any reference books.
After that, she had **50 books left**

** **
Reference books = unchanged anchor.
Books gone = 90 − 50 = **40, all story books**

** **
40 story books = 2/3 of her story books → story books = **60** Reference = 90 − 60 = **30 books**

**📝 Worked example:**
Mei Ling has **90 books** in total — some story, the rest reference.
She gave away **2/3 of her story books**.
She did NOT give away any reference books.
After that, she had **50 books left**.
How many reference books does she have?

**Step 1 — Spot the constant.**
Reference books = unchanged anchor.
Books gone = 90 − 50 = **40, all story books**.

**Step 2 — Reverse-engineer the missing original.**
40 story books = 2/3 of her story books → story books = **60**.
Reference = 90 − 60 = **30 books**.

💡 **The unlock:** because one part never moved, you only have ONE variable to solve — even though the problem mentions two quantities. Always identify the constant first, then the moving part is straightforward.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Equal total, different ratios (equalise!)
keyConcepts.body: Clue: "the total in Box 1 equals the total in Box 2" or "both contain the same number of …". The two ratios have DIFFERENT part-totals (e.g. 1:5 = 6 parts, 1:2 = 3 parts), so you must SCALE one of them so their part-totals match.
bullets:
**📝 Worked example:**
Box A: red : blue beads = **1 : 5** Box B: red : blue beads = **1 : 2** Both boxes hold the **same total** number of beads.
There are **30 red beads** in total across both boxes.

** **
Box A → 1 + 5 = **6 parts** Box B → 1 + 2 = **3 parts**

** **
LCM(6, 3) = 6. Box A is already 6 parts. Scale Box B by ×2 → **2 : 4** (now 6 parts)

** **
Box A: 1 red, 5 blue. Box B: 2 red, 4 blue.
Combined: red = 1 + 2 = **3**, blue = 5 + 4 = **9**, total = 12 parts

** **
3 parts red = 30 → 1 part = **10** → 9 parts blue = **90 blue beads**

**📝 Worked example:**
Box A: red : blue beads = **1 : 5**.
Box B: red : blue beads = **1 : 2**.
Both boxes hold the **same total** number of beads.
There are **30 red beads** in total across both boxes.
Find the total number of BLUE beads across both boxes.

**Step 1 — Find the part-totals.**
Box A → 1 + 5 = **6 parts**.
Box B → 1 + 2 = **3 parts**.
These don't match — but the real totals do. So 6 parts in A = 3 parts in B by count. Scale.

**Step 2 — Equalise totals using LCM.**
LCM(6, 3) = 6. Box A is already 6 parts. Scale Box B by ×2 → **2 : 4** (now 6 parts).

**Step 3 — Now per box (standard 6 parts):**
Box A: 1 red, 5 blue. Box B: 2 red, 4 blue.
Combined: red = 1 + 2 = **3**, blue = 5 + 4 = **9**, total = 12 parts.

**Step 4 — Use the given.**
3 parts red = 30 → 1 part = **10** → 9 parts blue = **90 blue beads**.
⚠️ **Common wrong move:** students add the ratios directly: 1+1 = 2 red, 5+2 = 7 blue, total 9 — wrong, because the two ratios have different part-sizes. Always equalise the totals first.

keyConcepts.title: Top 4 traps to avoid
keyConcepts.body: The most-missed mistakes across hidden-constant problems. Read these once before practice — they save 2-3 marks per paper.
bullets: ⚠️ ** 1 — Treating an internal transfer like an external loss.** Total stays constant
⚠️ ** 2 — Forgetting that "equal removal" preserves the DIFFERENCE, not the total.** gap
⚠️ ** 3 — In give-and-take, dividing the swing by 1 instead of 2.**
⚠️ ** 4 — Comparing two ratios without equalising their totals.**

⚠️ **Trap 1 — Treating an internal transfer like an external loss.**
If person A gave to person B, the stuff did NOT leave the problem. Total stays constant. Don't subtract from the total.

⚠️ **Trap 2 — Forgetting that "equal removal" preserves the DIFFERENCE, not the total.**
If the same number was taken from both parts, the gap between them is fixed, not the total.

⚠️ **Trap 3 — In give-and-take, dividing the swing by 1 instead of 2.**
Each $1 transferred moves the gap by $2 because one side loses while the other gains. Always divide the swing by 2.

⚠️ **Trap 4 — Comparing two ratios without equalising their totals.**
1 : 5 and 1 : 2 cannot be added or compared until you scale them to the same part-total (Pattern D).

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern C — One quantity unchanged
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Mei Ling has 90 books in total — some are story books and
the rest are reference books. She gave away 2/3 of her story
books. She did not give away any reference books. After that,
she had 50 books left. How many reference books does she have?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Reference books are the CONSTANT — they never changed. Books gone = 90 − 50 = 40, all story books. Those 40 are 2/3 of her story books → story books = 60. Reference = 90 − 60 = 30.

interactiveQuiz.label: Pattern D — Equalise ratios
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Container P has red and green marbles in the ratio 1 : 3.
Container Q has red and green marbles in the ratio 1 : 1.
Both containers hold the same TOTAL number of marbles. If
there are 6 red marbles in total across both containers, how
many GREEN marbles are there in total?
interactiveQuiz.explanation: P = 1+3 = 4 parts. Q = 1+1 = 2 parts. LCM = 4. Scale Q by ×2 → 2 : 2 (now 4 parts). Per standardised box: P has 1 red + 3 green; Q has 2 red + 2 green. Combined: red = 1+2 = 3 parts, green = 3+2 = 5 parts. 3 parts red = 6 → 1 part = 2 → 5 parts green = 10. (Check: each container = 4 parts = 8 marbles. P = 2 red + 6 green; Q = 4 red + 4 green. Total red = 6 ✓, total green = 10 ✓.)

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from real PSLE-style hidden-constant problems. Trains the reflex of running the 3-question check BEFORE drawing any model.
bullets: **mix of 10 questions** internal transfer, equal removal, one-constant, equalise-ratios
Wrong answers auto-saved reviewed in 1 week
AI marks partial credit
You'll tackle a **mix of 10 questions** across all four patterns — internal transfer, equal removal, one-constant, and equalise-ratios.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI marks your working step-by-step — partial credit if you spotted the right invariant but slipped on arithmetic.

keyWords.definition: When stuff moves from one party to another within the problem (not out of the problem). TOTAL stays constant — add the BEFORE quantities and use that as your fixed total. Includes give-and-take problems.
When A gives $X to B, the GAP between them changes by $2X (one loses while the other gains). Add before-gap and after-gap, divide by 2 to find the transfer. Still Pattern A — TOTAL is unchanged.
When the SAME amount is added to or removed from both parts of a ratio, the difference between them stays the same. Match the old-difference to the new-difference in units to solve.
The thing in the problem that never changes. Find it first — it becomes your fixed reference for solving for everything else.

When two parallel situations share an equal TOTAL but have different ratios (e.g. 1:5 and 1:2), scale them to a common part-total (LCM of denominators) BEFORE comparing or adding. Trying to add ratios directly without equalising gives wrong answers.

subTopics.label: Pattern A — Internal transfer (incl. give-and-take)
subTopics.description: Stuff moves from one party to another. TOTAL is constant. Add before-quantities, then apply the after-ratio. Give-and-take is the same pattern — each $1 transferred shifts the GAP by $2.

subTopics.label: Pattern B — Equal change to both
subTopics.description: Same amount added/removed from both parts. DIFFERENCE is constant. Match old-difference to new-difference in units.

subTopics.label: Pattern C — One quantity unchanged
subTopics.description: Only one of the two parts is affected. The other is your anchor. Solve for total-change from the part that did move.

subTopics.label: Pattern D — Equal total, equalise ratios
subTopics.description: Two situations share an equal TOTAL but different ratios. Scale both ratios to a common part-total (LCM) before combining or comparing.
